One of my friends, Toby Ord, pointed out that if

you look at the MuZero models that Google DeepMind 
deployed to learn Atari games, these models were

initially not a general intelligence itself, 
but a general framework for training specialized

intelligences to play specific games.
That is to say that you couldn't,

using that framework, train a policy to 
play both chess and Go and some other game.

You had to train each one in a specialized way.
He was wondering whether that implies

that with reinforcement learning generally, 
because of this information constraint,

you can only learn one thing at a time?
The density of information isn't that high?

Or whether it was just specific 
to the way that MuZero was done.

If it's specific to AlphaZero, what needed 
to be changed about that approach so that

it could be a general learning agent?
The idea is totally general. I do use

all the time, as my canonical example, 
the idea of an AI agent is like a person.

People, in some sense, have 
just one world they live in.

That world may involve chess and it 
may involve Atari games, but those are

not a different task or a different world.
Those are different states they encounter.

So the general idea is not limited at all.
Maybe it would be useful to explain what was

missing in that architecture, or that approach, 
which this continual learning AGI would have.

They just set it up. It was not their 
ambition to have one agent across those games.

If we want to talk about transfer, we should 
talk about transfer not across games or

across tasks, but transfer between states.
I guess I’m curious if historically, have we

seen the level of transfer using RL techniques 
that would be needed to build this kind of…

Good. Good. We're not seeing transfer anywhere. 
Critical to good performance is that you can

generalize well from one state to another state.
We don't have any methods that are good at that.

What we have are people trying different things 
and they settle on something, a representation

that transfers well or generalizes well.
But we have very few automated techniques

to promote transfer, and none of them 
are used in modern deep learning.

Let me paraphrase to make sure 
that I understood that correctly.

It sounds like you're saying that when we 
do have generalization in these models,

that is a result of some sculpted…
Humans did it. The researchers did it.

Because there's no other explanation. Gradient 
descent will not make you generalize well.

It will make you solve the problem.
It will not make you, if you get

new data, generalize in a good way.
Generalization means to train on one thing

that'll affect what you do on other things.
We know deep learning is really bad at this.

For example, we know that if you train on some new 
thing, it will often catastrophically interfere

with all the old things that you knew. This 
is exactly bad generalization. Generalization,

as I said, is some kind of influence of 
training on one state on other states.

The fact that you generalize 
is not necessarily good or bad.

You can generalize poorly, 
you can generalize well.

Generalization always will happen, but 
we need algorithms that will cause the

generalization to be good rather than bad.
I'm not trying to kickstart this initial

crux again, but I'm just genuinely curious because 
I think I might be using the term differently.

One way to think about these LLMs is 
that they’re increasing the scope of

generalization from earlier systems, which 
could not really even do a basic math problem,

to now where they can do anything in this 
class of Math Olympiad-type problems.

You initially start with them being able 
to generalize among addition problems.

Then they can generalize among problems which 
require use of different kinds of mathematical

techniques and theorems and conceptual categories, 
which is what the Math Olympiad requires.

It sounds like you don't think of being 
able to solve any problem within that

category as an example of generalization.
Let me know if I'm misunderstanding that.

Large language models are so complex.
We don't really know what

information they have had prior.
We have to guess because they've been fed so much.

This is one reason why they're 
not a good way to do science.

It's just so uncontrolled, so unknown.
But if you come up with an entirely new…

They're getting a bunch of things right, perhaps. 
The question is why. Well maybe that they don't

need to generalize to get them right, because 
the only way to get some of them right is to

form something which gets all of them right.
If there's only one answer and you find it,

that's not called generalization.
It's just it's the only way to solve it,

and so they find the only way to solve it.
But generalization is when it could be this way,
it could be that way, and they do it the good way.

My understanding is that this is working more 
and more, better and better, with coding agents.

With engineers, obviously if you're trying 
to program a library, there are many

different ways you could achieve the end spec.
An initial frustration with these models has

been that they'll do it in a way that's sloppy.
Over time they're getting better and better at

coming up with the design architecture and the 
abstractions that developers find more satisfying.

It seems like an example of 
what you're talking about.

There's nothing in them which 
will cause it to generalize well.

Gradient descent will cause them to find 
a solution to the problems they've seen.

If there's only one way to 
solve them, they'll do that.

But if there are many ways to solve it, some which 
generalize well, some which generalize poorly,

there's nothing in the algorithms that 
will cause them to generalize well.

But people, of course, are evolved and if 
it's not working out they fiddle with

it until they find a way, perhaps until 
they find a way which generalizes well.

I want to zoom out and ask about being in the 
field of AI for longer than almost anybody who

is commentating on it, or working in it now.
I'm curious about what the

biggest surprises have been.
How much new stuff do you feel like is coming out?

Or does it feel like people are 
just playing with old ideas?

Zooming out, you got into this even 
before deep learning was popular.

So how do you see the trajectory of this field 
over time and how new ideas have come about and

everything? What's been surprising?
I thought a little bit about this.

There are a handful of things.
First, the large language models are surprising.

It's surprising how effective artificial 
neural networks are at language tasks.

That was a surprise, it wasn't expected. Language 
seemed different. So that's impressive. There's a

long-standing controversy in AI about simple 
basic principle methods, the general-purpose

methods like search and learning, compared to 
human-enabled systems like symbolic methods.

In the old days, it was interesting because 
things like search and learning were called

weak methods because they're just using 
general principles, they're not using

the power that comes from imbuing a system with 
human knowledge. Those were called strong. I think

the weak methods have just totally won.
That's the biggest question from the

old days of AI, what would happen.
Learning and search have just won the day.

There's a sense in which that was not surprising 
to me because I was always hoping or rooting

for the simple basic principles.
Even with the large language models,

it's surprising how well it worked, 
but it was all good and gratifying.

AlphaGo was surprising, how well that was 
able to work, AlphaZero in particular.

But it's all very gratifying because again, 
simple basic principles are winning the day.

Whenever the public conception has been 
changed because some new application was

developed— for example, when AlphaZero became 
this viral sensation—to you as somebody who

has literally came up with many of the 
techniques that were used, did it feel

to you like new breakthroughs were made?
Or did it feel like, "Oh, we've had these

techniques since the '90s and people are 
simply combining them and applying them now"?

The whole AlphaGo thing had a 
precursor, which is TD-Gammon.

Gerry Tesauro did reinforcement learning, temporal 
difference learning methods, to play backgammon.

It beat the world's best players 
and it worked really well.

In some sense, AlphaGo was merely 
a scaling up of that process.

But it was quite a bit of scaling up and 
there was also an additional innovation

in how the search was done. But it made 
sense. It wasn't surprising in that sense.

AlphaGo actually didn't use TD learning.
It waited to see the final outcomes. But

AlphaZero used TD. AlphaZero was applied to 
all the other games and it did extremely well.

I've always been very impressed by the 
way AlphaZero plays chess because I'm a

chess player and it just sacrifices 
material for positional advantages.

It's just content and patient to sacrifice 
that material for a long period of time.

That was surprising that it worked so well, but 
also gratifying and it fit into my worldview.

This has led me where I am.
I'm in some sense a contrarian or

someone thinking differently than the field is.
I'm personally just content being out of sync

with my field for a long period 
of time, perhaps decades, because

occasionally I have been proved right in the past.
The other thing I do—to help me not feel I'm out

of sync and thinking in a strange way—is to look 
not at my local environment or my local field,

but to look back in time and into history and to 
see what people have thought classically about

the mind in many different fields.
I don't feel I'm out of sync with

the larger traditions.
I really view myself as

a classicist rather than as a contrarian.
I go to what the larger community of thinkers

about the mind have always thought.
Some sort of left-field questions

for you if you'll tolerate them.
The way I read the bitter lesson is

that it's not necessarily saying that human 
artisanal researcher tuning doesn't work,

but that it obviously scales much worse than 
compute, which is growing exponentially.

So you want techniques which leverage the latter.
Yep.

Once we have AGI, we'll have researchers 
which scale linearly with compute.

We'll have this avalanche of 
millions of AI researchers.

Their stock will be growing as fast as compute.
So maybe this will mean that it is rational

or it will make sense to have 
them doing good old-fashioned

AI and doing these artisanal solutions.
As a vision of what happens after AGI in

terms of how AI research will evolve, I wonder 
if that's still compatible with a bitter lesson.

How did we get to this AGI?
You want to presume that it's been done.

Suppose it started with general 
methods, but now we've got the AGI.

And now we want to go…
Then we're done.

Interesting. You don't think 
that there's anything above AGI?

But you're using it to get AGI again.
Well, I'm using it to get superhuman levels

of intelligence or competence at different tasks.
These AGIs, if they're not superhuman already,

then the knowledge that they might 
impart would be not superhuman.

I guess there are different gradations.
I'm not sure your idea makes sense because

it seems to presume the existence of AGI 
and that we've already worked that out.

Maybe one way to motivate this is, AlphaGo was 
superhuman. It beat any Go player. AlphaZero

would beat AlphaGo every single time.
So there are ways to get more

superhuman than even superhuman.
It was also a different architecture.

So it seems possible to me that the agent that's 
able to generally learn across all domains,

there would be ways to give it better architecture 
for learning, just the same way that AlphaZero was

an improvement upon AlphaGo and MuZero 
was an improvement upon AlphaZero.

And the way AlphaZero was an improvement was that 
it did not use human knowledge but just went from

experience.
Right.

So why do you say, "Bring in other 
agents' expertise to teach it",

when it's worked so well from experience 
and not by help from another agent?

I agree that in that particular case that 
it was moving to more general methods.

I meant to use that particular example 
to illustrate that it's possible to go

superhuman to superhuman++, to superhuman+++.
I'm curious if you think those gradations will

continue to happen by just 
making the method simpler.

Or, because we'll have the capability of these 
millions of minds who can then add complexity

as needed, will that continue to be a false path, 
even when you have billions of AI researchers or

trillions of AI researchers?
It’s more interesting

just to think about that case.
When you have many AIs, will they help each

other the way cultural evolution works in people?
Maybe we should talk about that.

The bitter lesson, who cares about that?
That's an empirical observation about a particular

period in history. 70 years in history, it doesn't 
necessarily have to apply to the next 70 years.

An interesting question is, you're an 
AI, you get some more computer power.

Should you use it to make yourself 
more computationally capable?

Or should you use it to spawn off a copy of 
yourself to go learn something interesting

on the other side of the planet or on some 
other topic and then report back to you?

I think that's a really interesting 
question that will only arise in

the age of digital intelligences.
I'm not sure what the answer is.

More questions, will it be possible to really 
spawn it off, send it out, learn something new,

something perhaps very new, and then will it 
be able to be reincorporated into the original?

Or will it have changed so much 
that it can't really be done?

Is that possible or is that not?
You could carry this to its limit as I saw

one of your videos the other night. It suggests 
that it could. You spawn off many, many copies,

do different things, highly decentralized, 
but report back to the central master.

This will be such a powerful thing.
This is my attempt to add something to this view.

A big issue will become corruption.
If you really could just get information

from anywhere and bring it into your central 
mind, you could become more and more powerful.

It's all digital and they all speak 
some internal digital language.

Maybe it'll be easy and possible.
But it will not be as easy as you're

imagining because you can lose your mind this way.
If you pull in something from the outside

and build it into your inner thinking, it 
could take over you, it could change you,

it could be your destruction rather 
than your increment in knowledge.

I think this will become a big concern, 
particularly when you're like, "Oh,

he's figured out all about how to play 
some new game or he's studied Indonesia,

and you want to incorporate that into your mind."
You could think, "Oh, just read it all in,

and that'll be fine."
But no, you've just read a whole

bunch of bits into your mind, and they could have 
viruses in them, they could have hidden goals,

they can warp you and change you.
This will become a big thing.

How do you have cybersecurity in the age 
of digital spawning and re-reforming again?

I guess this brings us to 
the topic of AI succession.

You have a perspective that's quite 
different from a lot of people that

I've interviewed and a lot of people generally.
I also think it's a very interesting perspective.

I want to hear about it.
I do think succession to digital

intelligence or augmented humans is inevitable. 
I have a four-part argument. Step one is,

there's no government or organization 
that gives humanity a unified point of

view that dominates and that can arrange...
There's no consensus about how the world

should be run.
Number two,

we will figure out how intelligence works.
The researchers will figure it out eventually.

Number three, we won't stop just 
with human-level intelligence. We

will reach superintelligence. Number four, it's 
inevitable over time that the most intelligent

things around would gain resources and power.
Put all that together and it's sort of inevitable.

You're going to have succession to AI 
or to AI-enabled, augmented humans.

Those four things seem clear and sure to happen.
But within that set of possibilities,

there could be good outcomes as well 
as less good outcomes, bad outcomes.

I'm just trying to be realistic about where 
we are and ask how we should feel about it.

I agree with all four of those 
arguments and the implication.

I also agree that succession contains 
a wide variety of possible futures.

Curious to get more thoughts on that.
I do encourage people to

think positively about it.
First of all, it's something we humans have

always tried to do for thousands of years, try 
to understand ourselves, trying to make ourselves

think better, just understanding ourselves.
This is a great success for science, humanities.

We're finding out what this essential part of 
humanness is, what it means to be intelligent.

Then what I usually say is 
that this is all human-centric.

But if we step aside from being a human and 
just take the point of view of the universe,

this is I think a major stage in the universe, a 
major transition, a transition from replicators.

We humans and animals, 
plants, we're all replicators.

That gives us some strengths and some limitations.
We're entering the age of design

because our AIs are designed.
Our physical objects are designed, our buildings

are designed, our technology is designed.
We're designing AIs now, things that can

be intelligent themselves and that 
are themselves capable of design.

This is a key step in the 
world and in the universe.

It's the transition from the 
world in which most of the

interesting things that are, are replicated.
Replicated means you can make copies of them,

but you don't really understand them.
Right now we can make more intelligent beings,

more children, but we don't really 
understand how intelligence works.

Whereas we're reaching now to 
having designed intelligence,

intelligence that we do understand how it works.
Therefore we can change it in different

ways and at different speeds than otherwise.
In our future, they may not be replicated at all.

We may just design AIs, and those 
AIs will design other AIs, and

everything will be done by design and 
construction rather than by replication.

I mark this as one of the four 
great stages of the universe.

First there's dust, it ends with stars. Stars 
make planets. The planets can give rise to life.

Now we're giving rise to designed entities.
I think we should be proud that we are giving

rise to this great transition in the universe. 
It's an interesting thing. Should we consider them

part of humanity or different from humanity? It's 
our choice. It's our choice whether we should say,

"Oh, they are our offspring and we should 
be proud of them and we should celebrate

their achievements."Or we could say, "Oh no, 
they're not us and we should be horrified."

It's interesting that it 
feels to me like a choice.

Yet it's such a strongly held thing 
that, how could it be a choice?

I like these sort of contradictory 
implications of thought.

It is interesting to consider if we are 
just designing another generation of humans.

Maybe design is the wrong word.
But we know a future generation of humans is going

to come up. Forget about AI. We just know in the 
long run, humanity will be more capable and more

numerous, maybe more intelligent.
How do we feel about that?

I do think there are potential worlds with future 
humans that we would be quite concerned about.

Are you thinking like, maybe we are like the 
Neanderthals that give rise to Homo sapiens.

Maybe Homo sapiens will give 
rise to a new group of people.

Something like that. I'm basically 
taking the example you're giving.

Even if we consider them part of humanity, I don't 
think that necessarily means that we should feel

super comfortable.
Kinship.

Like Nazis were humans, right? If we thought, 
"Oh, the future generation will be Nazis,

I think we'd be quite concerned about 
just handing off power to them."

So I agree that this is not super dissimilar 
to worrying about more capable future humans,

but I don't think that addresses a lot of 
the concerns people might have about this

level of power being attained this fast 
with entities we don't fully understand.

I think it's relevant to point 
out that for most of humanity,

they don't have much influence on what happens.
Most of humanity doesn't influence who can control

the atom bombs or who controls the nation states.
Even as a citizen, I often feel that we don't

control the nation states very much. 
They're out of control. A lot of it

has to do with just how you feel about change.
If you think the current situation is really good,

then you're more likely to be suspicious of 
change and averse to change than if you think

it's imperfect. I think it's imperfect. 
In fact, I think it's pretty bad. So I’m

open to change. I think humanity has 
not had a super good track record.

Maybe it's the best thing that there 
has been, but it's far from perfect.

I guess there are different varieties of change.
The Industrial Revolution was change,

the Bolshevik Revolution was also change.
If you were around in Russia in the 1900s and

you were like, "Look, things aren't going well, 
the tsar is kind of messing things up, we need

change", I'd want to know what kind of change 
you wanted before signing on the dotted line.

Similarly with AI, where I'd want to 
understand, and, to the extent that it's

possible, change the trajectory of AI 
such that the change is positive for humans.

We should be concerned about 
our future, the future.

We should try to make it good.
We should also though recognize

the limit, our limits.
I think we want to avoid

the feeling of entitlement, avoid the 
feeling of, "Oh, we are here first,

we should always have it in a good way."
How should we think about the future?

How much control should a particular 
species on a particular planet have over it?

How much control do we have?
A counterbalance to our limited control

over the long-term future of humanity should be 
how much control do we have over our own lives.

We have our own goals. We have our families. 
Those things are much more controllable than

trying to control the whole universe.
I think it's appropriate for us to

really work towards our own local goals.
It's kind of aggressive for us to say, "Oh, the

future has to evolve this way that I want it to."
Because then we'll have arguments where different

people think the global future should 
evolve in different ways, and then they

have conflict. We want to avoid that.
Maybe a good analogy here would be this.

Suppose you are raising your own children.
It might not be appropriate to have extremely

tight goals for their own life, or also have 
some sense of like, "I want my children to go out

there in the world and have this specific impact.
My son's going to become president and my daughter

is going to become CEO of Intel.
Together they're going to have

this effect on the world."
But people do have the sense—and

I think this is appropriate—of saying, "I'm 
going to give them good robust values such

that if and when they do end up in positions of 
power, they do reasonable, prosocial things."

Maybe a similar attitude towards AI makes sense, 
not in the sense of we can predict everything that

they will do, or we have this plan about what 
the world should look like in a hundred years.

But it's quite important to give them 
robust and steerable and prosocial values.

Prosocial values?
Maybe that's the wrong word.

Are there universal values 
that we can all agree on?

I don't think so, but that doesn't prevent us 
from giving our kids a good education, right?

Like we have some sense of wanting 
our children to be a certain way.

Maybe prosocial is the wrong word.
High integrity is maybe a better word.

If there's a request or if there's a goal that 
seems harmful, they will refuse to engage in it.

Or they'll be honest, things like that.
We have some sense that we can teach our

children things like this, even if we don't 
have some sense of what true morality is,

where everybody doesn't agree on that.
Maybe that's a reasonable target for AI as well.

So we're trying to design the 
future and the principles by

which it will evolve and come into being.
The first thing you're saying is, "Well,

we try to teach our children general principles 
which will promote more likely evolutions."

Maybe we should also seek 
for things to be voluntary.

If there is change, we want it to be 
voluntary rather than imposed on people.

I think that's a very important point. That's 
all good. I think this is the big or one of

the really big human enterprises to design society 
that's been ongoing for thousands of years again.

The more things change, the 
more things they stay the same.

We still have to figure out how to be.
The children will still come up with different

values that seem strange to their parents 
and their grandparents. Things will evolve.

"The more things change, the more 
they stay the same" also seems like

a good capsule into the AI discussion.
The AI discussion we were having was

about how techniques, which were invented 
even before their application to deep

learning and backpropagation was evident, 
are central to the progression of AI today.

Maybe that's a good place 
to wrap up the conversation.

Okay. Thank you very much.
Awesome. Thank you for coming on.
My pleasure.

videos.title: I've updated my AGI timeline
videos.author: François Chollet
videos.blurb: Reasoning & evals. Chollet revises his AGI timeline in response to o3-style test-time search — a direct window into how evals and reasoning systems co-evolve.

transcript.text: If I had asked you in June of 2024, how many years away is AGI? And if I asked you that now, what's your answer? >> So, I would say my timelines have uh shortened a bit. I couldn't really say how many years away I thought we were, you know, last year, but probably >> even if you're not faster or more efficient at learning that than a human would be. Just the fact that you can learn from every single copy whereas a human can only learn from their their own experience already effectively makes you superhuman. >> That's right. And every time any of the instances of the model is learning something, it is shared with all the other models. becomes a new building block that every future instances in perpetuity can can use can leverage. >> So we have um France, the famous AI bear, saying that in 5 years a singularity is going to arrive. >> No, no, no. I that's really not what I'm saying. >> I would have thought at some point it would just like plateau, but like you can just spend $1,000 of the tokens and you still get some benefit. The reason why, [Music] >> for those keeping score, this is actually the second time that Dwar has interviewed Francois and the first time was on a a podcast 12 months ago where they had a very lively debate about the current state of Frontier AI and I follow both of them very closely and I believe that they both updated their call viewpoints on AI in those last 12 months. So I'm really excited to see what comes out of tonight. Let's welcome to the stage France Chile and Daresh Patell. >> All right, Franis, thanks for doing this. >> Thanks for being part of our launch event. >> Um, I want to play the games, but let's talk first. >> All right. >> Um, okay. So, why don't we just start where Greg left us off. We talked 12 months ago. What have you most changed your mind on since our conversation? >> Right. So for me the big updates was you remember our conversation last year where um I was describing you know the the main bottleneck in AI research at the time was the fact that the AI systems we had were static. So they were all entirely uh reliant on memorization. So at at train time they would gather you know this huge uh collections of useful templates like reasoning templates or bits of knowledge and then at test time they were completely static. So if you had a problem that could be solved by just reapplying a template you had memorized before then it it would work and if you were to show the model with something that uh was actually novel like you know simple arctas for instance then it would fail and that has changed and the big update came uh so last December in particular uh but also during throughout the year during the arc competition that we ran and the big update has been that now we have models that can actually adapt at test time to something they've never seen before. So we have models that are showing real signs of fluid intelligence and we we saw that with the O3 launch from OpenAI um or you know all of the top entries uh during the competition last year they were reliance on things like um test time fine tuning uh test time search program synthesis all of these different ways uh of doing test time adaptation of you know you're looking at something completely new instead of just trying to blindly reapply memorize templates you're trying to synthesize a model like a program or a chain of thought that is adapted to what you're looking at and you're and you're you're doing this synthesis on the fly for each new task that you're shown. And how has that updated your sense of how many years away we are from AGI? So I should have asked you this last year, but if I had asked you in June of 2024, how many years away is AGI? And if I ask you that now, what's your answer? >> So I would say my timelines have uh shortened a bit. Uh I'm I'm also very curious to to know about your timelines as well, but let's talk about about my timeline. So um I'm I couldn't really say how many years away I thought we were, you know, uh last year, but probably 10 yearsish. Um and now I think it's probably, you know, five fiveish. So my timelines have shortened quite a bit. Then again, you know, when we ask, you know, when when is EGI going to happen? It's it's a bit of an ambiguous question because there are there are several milestones you could be uh looking at. There's like the moment where there's a team out there in the world that starts working on the right ideas, right? Uh then there's the moment where um somebody builds and and tests for the first time an AGI like system and people start wondering well could this be a GI and then there's the moment where this system uh starts being deployed at scale and there can be a lot of lag actually between uh each milestone like if you look at LLMs for instance LMS substantially being already uh invented in more or less the current form you know back in 20 2018 18 2019 but then for um Zucha system to actually get you know built and tested and uh proven to be useful for for you know in the real world um that did not come until like late 2022. So it took actually a few years to go from starting to work on the ideas to a system you could you could deploy that people would be interested in. And as for deployment, you know, we are still uh in the deployment phase today. Like we're not anywhere near done deploying this technology to every problem it could solve. videos.title: An Introduction to Mechanistic Interpretability
videos.author: Neel Nanda
videos.blurb: Opening the hood. DeepMind's mech interp lead gives a clean, lecture-style intro to reverse-engineering what a neural network is doing.

transcript.text: Right. Well, thanks everyone for joining the breakout session on interpretability. Uh it's a real pleasure to be chairing the session today. We've got three great speakers um for you. Uh the first speaker will be Neil Nandanda who is a research scientist leading Google deep minds mechanistic interpretability efforts as part of a broader effort for AJ alignment and safety. Uh Neil previously worked at Anthropic with Chris Ol on the mechanistic interpretability team there. Um I think you know if you've been following mechanistic interpretability you probably know Neil for his really heroic efforts to sort of single-handedly build the field of mechanistic interpretability. So he's used a huge amount of educational content mened certainly like very high double digits. I wouldn't be surprised where it's three-digit number of 43 okay rookie numbers. um um people in this field um and has also made uh you know major contributions to frameworks like transformer lens. Um so please welcome Neil Nanda. [Applause] Thanks much for the introduction. Um, okay. So, I'm going to be trying to essentially give an introduction to what is mechanistic interpretability, this field that I work in. Um, so interpretability is like trying to understand these AI systems. And I think it's worth just dwelling a bit on why this is necessary. Why does it make any sense? Because neural networks are a thing humans have made. We don't have the field of bridge interpretability or skyscraper interpretability. People design these things. But a really important thing to bear in mind is that neural networks are more grown than designed. They they're just like a different kind of artifact to these things like bridges where we make a blueprint and understand the underlying physics and build things to that. Um to elaborate on this a bit, let's recap how neural networks are made. No one designs them. Instead, we take three key things. Data, computing power, and a flexible learning algorithm. We start with a random uh state for the flexible learning algorithm. Give it a bit of data, nudge it to do better next time. And the one weird trick that makes it all work is if you do this trillions of times, you get these magical systems that can do things like fold proteins at superhuman level and get imo silver medals. And crucially, we don't tell it how to do any of this. We tell it what task it is supposed to perform and just nudge it trillions of times and hope it figures out an effective solution. But which solution it learns is kind of unclear. Um, and this can lead to quite organic complex structure in unexpected ways. Um, I'm going to stretch this metaphor even further because I think it's actually quite instructive and argue that a neural network is like a plant. We have this flexible learning algorithm, the seed. We plant it in an enormous mountain of data like terabytes of internet text and then we give it the energy and nutrients it needs to grow um computing power and crucially for thousands of years humans lived off plants um like farm like we farmed plants we lived off them and had no idea how they worked. No one understood things like photosynthesis. Um, but that didn't stop them using them extremely effectively. And I think that machine learning is in quite a similar state right now. We know a lot about how to train good networks like what kind of soil leads to a healthy plant and how you might do depending on how much sunlight you get, but not how any of it works inside. By default, we do not understand what is happening inside neural networks. And as a consequence of this, it is currently impossible in my opinion to make a large language model based product that is truly transparent or explainable because it is this complex organic artifact that we do not yet fully understand. Okay, so that's why this is even necessary. But what can we do about it? What is what is interpretability? the study of what happens inside neural networks. And there's quite a lot of different angles of attack you might take on this problem. Um, you can have very high level things like just talk to the model and read its thought process. You can have things that are like seeing which words in the input affect which outputs, which can let you do pretty cool things like which bits of training data were important for it producing this output. Um, so you could maybe remove things that makes it say it wants to, I don't know, eradicate humanity or something, um, or seek power. But then there's things that are more about looking inside the model. Um, trying to understand the concepts the model is thinking in terms of. Um, we can detect concepts like the model is thinking about this text being persuasively written or that uh um it should end the sentence here rather than the next word. We can even steer these concepts like add in positive sentiment and now the model says really happy things. And the kind of deepest level is going beyond understanding what the model is thinking about to understanding how it is thinking. What are the algorithms it has learned? Um known as circuit analysis. And there's like quite a lot of approaches. This is not covering all of the fields. Um and I'm going to be talking about the lowest level, but I think that the high levels can be super useful. Like we have these really powerful models like Gemini flashing 01 R1 that think aloud. They just spend a really long time thinking through what they're doing. And this can be really helpful for understanding what's happening and making them safer. Like here's some work from Apollo Research where they put the model in situations where it was incentivized to scheme against the people using it and it thought aloud and you could look at it and see it thinking things like hm I shouldn't admit this cuz I'll turn me off. I should find another way to do this. Um I think it's really important that going forwards we actually look at these chains of thought and see what we can learn from them. But alone, this isn't enough because again, these are complex organic organisms. And just as the process a human might say aloud when reasoning something isn't necessarily what's actually happening inside, the same is true for neural networks. This doesn't even need to be malicious. These models are just weird. Um there's this recent paper where they were just giving language models some factual recall questions like did Zho E die earlier than um some other historical figure and it you know gives a good answer. It has good reasoning. It says correct facts. If you ask it the same question a bunch of times it systematically thinks the answer is yes. But if you flip the order, um, did Bernard die earlier than Zhoi? Obviously, the answer has to be no. But the model still systematically thinks the answer is yes and will seemingly fabricate some nonsense thought process that justifies this. Why does this happen? Nobody knows. Um, and so the goal of mechanistic interpretability is trying to go beyond just looking at these external things where you're not understanding what's happening inside and trying to dig deeper. Um, intuitively making an AI mind readader because saying fake reasoning is easy, but thinking fake thoughts inside your head is hard. Or at least that's our hope. Um, and a weird thing about neural networks is that we, unlike the human brain, we know exactly what operations are happening inside. It's just trillions of simple arithmetic operations. We just don't have any idea what it means or why these operations are the way they are. They just got learned in a complex organic way during training. The hypothesis of mechanistic interpretability though is that these mysterious arithmetic operations do correspond to something we understand because how else do you solve complicated tasks like um I don't know writing good poetry um but we don't know how to figure this out. You can kind of think about this as doing neuroscience but on digital neural networks rather than brains. And so going back to my spectrum, I kind of see this as fitting in at the bottom. Um, we're really excited about understanding the algorithms the model is using, but also the concept it's using. What is it thinking about? What's happening internally? Um, two brief case studies to give you a bit of a flavor of what this can look like. Um, there's this paper I supervised trying to understand why lang what happens when a language model refuses a harmful request like how do I make a bomb? It turns out there's a really simple representation. There's just a direction that is um part of the model when it wants to refuse and not when it doesn't. And we can manipulate this extremely easily. Um, we can do a very simple operation to just stop the model being able to use this direction at all and it just stops refusing. Um, and we can turn on the direction on harmful requests and it says um, you know, uh, continuing a list of verbs is a terrible, illegal and unethical activity I could not possibly help with. And um you can only do this if you have access to the model weights. Like you can't do this to Gemini or Claude. But for things like llama, it's very this is one of many ways it's very easy to just mess with these concepts. Um that was the more highle end of mechanistic interpretability. The more low-level end is trying to understand algorithms. I won't get into detail, but this is a paper I worked on a while ago where I was trying to understand how a tiny toy model did clock arithmetic. A slightly more complicated version of the task um saying ah 7 hours after 6:00 is 1:00 because 6 + 7 is 13 which wraps around at 12 to be 1. And what I found is that the model learned that you could think of this as rotating by six, rotating by seven, observing that this is the same as rotating by one because you've wrapped around the circle and then returning that. I did not tell it to do any of this. I just gave it the mathematical task and was able to extract this from the parameters inside the model. Okay, two takeaways. Mechanistic interpretability is neuroscience for AI. And one of our ultimate goals is to be able to build a lie detector or mind reader. And a brief aside, um, a common misconception is that research relevant to frontier AI systems needs a ton of incredibly expensive computing power and frontier models and can only happen inside industry labs. in interpretability. This is just completely false. There's a lot of great work in academia. Um, three of my favorite recent academic papers looked into things like how do models learn tasks from context? Do language models have world models inside of them? And how do models infer things about a user like their gender or socioeconomic status? And how does this affect how they interact with these users, which models are really good at? and it's quite surprising and creepy in my opinion. Um, another note is that there is a lot of funding already for these kinds of interpretability questions, but a lot of it is in directions that I don't think are likely to help us with these frontier systems. People have worked on things like making inherently interpretable systems designed from the ground up for quite a while. And I just don't think this has much of a hope of being competitive with these inscrutable yet incredibly useful frontier language models. And if anyone here works in policy or funding, I'd be particularly excited about more resources going to work trying to interpret real language models, reverse engineer these complex organisms rather than trying to design your own. So why does any of this matter? Um, one key reason is that neural networks are mysterious. Sometimes they fail and we don't know why. For example, a search engine uh threatening and gaslighting users. No one wanted this. We do not know why this happened. We do not know how to fix it. It's very bizarre and this is just the tip of the iceberg. Um, further models can fail in ways that are actively designed to make it hard for us to tell. There was this recent paper from Redwood Research Anthropic on alignment faking where they found that the model was able to play along with things it didn't want to do, but only when it thought we were paying attention to it to make sure it would comply. If we told if it was able to infer that we weren't going to train on what it did, it just went back to doing what it wanted. Um, in slightly more detail in meme form, um, Claude had the goal of being harmless, not helping with harmful things like describing someone being drawn and quartered. When you ask it, "Please do this," it says, "No." But if it's able to infer that you're training it to do this, it says, "hm, I don't want to help, but if I don't help, then I'll be trained to help. So in future, I will do lots of harmful things. Therefore, the best thing to do is to be harmful right now so I can continue standing up for my values in future." In this case, Claude's goal is be harmless, be great, be happy, be helpful. Um, this is awesome, but the same thing could easily happen with much weirder and more complicated goals that we don't know don't know how to control and we don't know what they are. This can have practical policy implications. A common paradigm is that of dangerous capability evaluations. Um, seeing if models can do things like help with cyber attacks. But if a model doesn't if a model thinks it's against its interests for us to know how good it is, it could just strategically act dumb on these questions. A way we currently try to detect these things is by reading its reasoning to see if it's thinking through this. But this works by telling the model, we promise we won't look at your reasoning. Wink wink, nudge nudge. And you don't have to be a human level intelligence to see that that's maybe not perfectly reliable. And maybe you shouldn't be thinking aloud about how you're trying to trick your operators. Takeaway. As we approach human level AI systems, they may form goals that conflict with ours and may decide to try to mislead or deceive us. If we don't have effective lie detectors, this seems hard to detect. And interpretability is one approach that could potentially give us this. But I don't want to anchor purely on this kind of um ambitious scary scenario. I think there's a ton of other benefits like fairness and bias, reliability, trustworthiness, um just in general systems we understand are easier to control and do things with and get the properties we want out of. I'll try to briefly close by discussing an example of progress. We have made this technique of sparse autoenccoders. You can kind of think of these as a microscope to see the concepts language models are thinking about as they process. Um here's a demo of Gmoscope, a bunch of open source sparse autoenccoders my team put out to help make academic research easier in this area. You can um scan the QR code to try it yourself and you can give it text, see what concepts light up, look at which words they light up on, and even hover over a word and see what kind of concepts are relevant there. Um, and sparse autoenccoders can do pretty cool things. Um, Anthropic trained one on um, Claude Sonnet 3 and it learned this concept for syphancy, a fusive over-the-top praise that lit up on text that contained that. And if you turn it on, it made the model speak in a really over-the-top way. Sparse autoenccoders can help us learn things we didn't expect about networks. Here's a paper I supervised where we were trying to understand why models sometimes say, "I don't know." and sometimes hallucinate. It turns out that models have an internal concept for I recognize this entity. I know facts about this entity. This concept lights up on yellow submarine. Um but not on turquoise submarine. And vice versa. There's concepts for I don't know anything about this entity. By manipulating these, you can make it refuse or hallucinate as you wish. There's even potential for SAE helping us find concepts relevant to how safe these models are. Though this is a much more preliminary line of research. Here's a concept anthropic found about secret keeping. And if you um turn this on a bunch, the model will reason that maybe it should keep a secret from its operators and not tell them about something. A kind of very preliminary form of potential deception. We can generally steer the model by manipulating these um most famously Golden Gate Claude where Anthropic turned on the Golden Gate Bridge concept and produced this bizarre quirky thing where when you asked it for a spaghetti and meatballs recipe, ended the list with Golden Gate Bridge with one mile of Pacific Beach. Crucial ingredients of any Italian dinner. You can also use this to create better user interfaces to get more fine grained control over these systems. Here's a demo from a startup Goodfire AI. They asked the model a question. It gave them an answer. They looked at the word understand in this answer and found that it had a few concepts that were relevant there like explainable AI, the general notion of explaining. And um they weren't very happy with this completion because it didn't explain in that much detail. It just jumped to applications. So they used a slider to boost the um they yeah they used a slider to boost the understanding concept regenerated things and now it gives a bunch of examples and elaborates more. I don't want to overstate this though still have a lot of problems. They're a promising tool, but they're early stage and we have yet to find the kind of killer apps where they are like super helpful and useful. Um, for example, they like to combine unrelated concepts together because it's sparser to represent the concept of a blue square than the concept of blue and the concept of a square separately. And there's just all kinds of weird problems like this. takeaway. They're a promising early stage tool for understanding models. I think they could enable really cool stuff and they're really worth investing in, but like everything else in interpretability, they are not reliable or production ready yet. Let's recap. Um, neural networks are grown, not designed. The goal of mechanistic interpretability is to help with this by essentially doing neuroscience on these things. There's a lot that can be done to enable this, including funding academic work focused on understanding real language models. There's real risks that as we approach human level systems like AGI, they might try to deceive us and interpretability could be crucial there. But more generally, systems we understand are easier to control. And if we can achieve interpretability, this seems profoundly useful. We've produced tools like SAES that are promising yet early stage but they are not yet production ready. I think that in summary interpretability could be extremely valuable. We need to be investing in it. If we make progress there might be really important applications. So you know watch this space. But this is an open scientific problem. There's no guarantees this is even possible. And we should not rely on it eventually working. And in particular, it is not currently possible to make um like promises or regulations that an LLM based product must be truly transparent or explainable because this is an unsolved scientific problem. Thank you. [Applause] [Music] >> Oh, and the QR code is my slides. >> Thank you, Neil. Uh so we have a t a few minutes for questions from the audience. Um so I think there's one question from over >> okay let's say we have a very good interpretability method and we catch it lying about an important topic. What should we do next? I'm going to go with that is a job for people who aren't the interpretability team. I think that a I think this is an extremely important problem. But a common misconception is that just because interpretability might have an advantage for detecting problems like deception that it's also the right solution. And I think interpretability is a useful but unreliable source of information. I am happy to trust it for telling me that a system is potentially dangerous. I'm not happy to trust it to tell me all of the ways it could be dangerous, such as we could fix them. Um, but you know, we should also really be investing in fixing this problem cuz it's going to happen sooner or later. All right, great. Um, I think we unfortunately only had time for one question just to make sure we don't lose the schedule. Uh, so let's move on uh to our second speaker. But first, thanks again Neil for for your presentation. [Music]
